answer:
**King's Enforcement of Debt Recovery**

Yājñavalkya's *Smṛti* (Ch. 2) delivers the procedural rule in tight, aphoristic prose, emphasizing the king's direct intervention with fixed surcharges on recovery:

> The debtor shall be made by the king to pay ten percent of the amount recovered; and the creditor who has recovered his money shall be made to pay five percent.[^1]

Manu’s *Smṛti* (Ch. 8) expands this into a fuller judicial process, stressing proof (documents, witnesses) before enforcement, while allowing creditor self-help:

> Being urged by a creditor for the recovery of his property from a debtor, he should make the debtor pay the creditor's property, after it has been proven. By whatever means a creditor may recover his own property, by those same means he should seize the debtor and make him pay.[^2]

[^1]: Yājñavalkya *Smṛti*, Ch. 2, [p-4c4f1e](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-4c4f1e).
[^2]: Manu *Smṛti*, Ch. 8, [paragraphs](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/08/translation/#p-803bc3) covering Mn 8.47–49 (enforcement after proof; self-recovery permitted).
